summary: O'Neil examines the failure of online courses at San Jose State University, where 56-76% of students failed basic classes, and uses this as a springboard to question the true purpose of college education. Rather than seeing the high failure rates as surprising, she's impressed anyone passed given the 'light-weight connection' of online learning. Through an anecdote about a Yale graduate who skipped classes but built a valuable business network with classmates, she argues that education's primary value lies in relationship-building rather than content delivery.
key_points: San Jose State suspended Udacity online courses after failure rates of 56-76% in basic undergraduate classes
The 'light-weight connection' of online courses makes even passing them an achievement
A Yale graduate admitted he learned nothing in classes but built lasting business relationships with classmates
Education's value may be more about making connections than acquiring knowledge
Personal connections made in college can have lasting professional impacts, like introductions to mentors and advisors
money_quotes: Personally, I'm impressed that so many people passed them considering how light-weight the connection is in such course experiences.
It all depends on what you were expecting, I guess. It begs the question of what college is for anyway.
He never learned a thing in classes, that in fact he skipped most of his classes to hang out with his buddies. He somehow thought MOOCs would be a fine replacement for that experience.
When I asked him whether he still knew any of his buddies from college, he acknowledged that he does business with them all the time.
This confirms my theory that education is more about making connection than education per se.
key_insight: MOOCs fail to replicate the real value of college, which lies not in content delivery but in the lasting personal and professional connections formed through shared physical presence.
